meta.title: Dermatology Practice Software | Mohs Efficiency & Biopsy Tracking | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to dermatology practice operations. 25% faster Mohs throughput. Zero biopsy results lost. Cosmetic flow separation. Biologic authorization tracking.

hero.subhead: Your Mohs days run on stage turnover — 25% faster is one more case. Your biopsies need tracking from specimen to patient notification. Your cosmetic patients need a different experience than medical patients. clinIQ optimizes Mohs efficiency, tracks every biopsy, and separates cosmetic flow.

sections.content:
Dermatology practices are operationally complex: high-volume medical visits, procedure-heavy Mohs days, cosmetic services, and pathology tracking all run simultaneously.

Mohs efficiency: Mohs surgery revenue depends on stage turnover. Time between excision and slide review is time the patient waits and the surgeon isn't operating. Faster turnover = more cases per day.

Biopsy tracking: Dermatology sends hundreds of biopsies monthly. Each needs result tracking, patient notification, and appropriate follow-up. Biopsies that fall through cracks create liability and harm patients.

Cosmetic vs medical: Cosmetic patients expect a boutique experience. Medical patients expect efficient care. Running both through the same flow satisfies neither.

Biologic authorization: Psoriasis and eczema biologics require prior auth, ongoing monitoring, and renewal tracking. Patients lose access when auths expire.

High volume: Dermatology runs more patients per day than most specialties — 40-60 patients isn't unusual. Flow efficiency matters more.

This guide covers systems that address each challenge.

Mohs surgery is a multi-stage process:

1. Excision: Surgeon removes visible tumor plus margin
2. Processing: Tissue processed and slides prepared (15-30 minutes)
3. Slide review: Surgeon examines slides under microscope
4. Decision: Clear margins → proceed to repair. Positive margins → return to step 1.

Most Mohs cases require 1-2 stages. Complex cases may require 3-4.

The efficiency opportunity is in the gaps:

- Time between excision completion and slide delivery
- Time between slide delivery and surgeon review
- Time between surgeon decision and patient communication
- Time between clearance and repair initiation

**The throughput math:**

Average Mohs day: 8 patients, 1.5 stages average = 12 stages.

If average stage turnaround is 45 minutes, total stage time: 9 hours.

If average stage turnaround is 35 minutes, total stage time: 7 hours.

2 hours saved = capacity for 2-3 additional patients.

At $1,500-2,500 per Mohs case, that's $3,000-7,500/day additional capacity.

**What clinIQ does for Mohs:**

Stage tracking: Excision complete → In processing → Slides ready → Under review → Decision made. Every stage visible.

Time alerts: 'Stage 1 in processing for 35 minutes (expected: 25).' Delays surface immediately.

Patient status: Which patients are waiting for slides? Which are cleared and ready for repair? Which are returning for additional stage?

Repair queue: Cleared patients queued for repair. Repair room availability visible. Surgeon transitions efficiently.

Histotech visibility: If using in-house histotechs, their workload and progress is visible. Bottlenecks identified early.

sections.h2: Biopsy Result Tracking: Zero Lost Results
sections.content:
A busy dermatology practice sends 200-400 biopsies monthly to pathology. Each biopsy needs:

- Specimen logged and sent
- Result received
- Result reviewed by provider
- Patient notified
- Follow-up scheduled (if indicated)

Without systematic tracking, biopsies fall through cracks:

'Did we get the path results on Mr. Johnson's mole biopsy?'
'I think so... let me check... actually I'm not sure.'

Meanwhile, Mr. Johnson has melanoma and hasn't been told.

**The liability math:**

Missed melanoma diagnosis is one of the most common and costly dermatology malpractice claims. Average settlement: $500,000+. 

Systematic biopsy tracking isn't just operational efficiency — it's risk management.

**What clinIQ does for biopsy tracking:**

Specimen pipeline: Every biopsy tracked from collection through patient notification.

- Biopsy performed (date, site, clinical description)
- Specimen sent to lab
- Result received from lab
- Result reviewed by provider
- Patient notified
- Follow-up scheduled (if needed)

Aging alerts: 'Biopsy performed 12 days ago, result not received.' Problems surface before patients call.

Result review queue: Which path results are waiting for physician review? Color-coded by urgency (dysplastic nevus vs squamous cell vs melanoma).

Patient notification documentation: Notification method, date, staff member. Audit trail.

**The unexpected result protocol:**

Some results require urgent action (melanoma, aggressive SCC). clinIQ can flag specific diagnoses for immediate escalation rather than routine review queue.

Dermatology practices often run medical and cosmetic services. The patient expectations are completely different.

Medical patients: Efficient care, minimal wait, clinical environment.

Cosmetic patients: Boutique experience, personalized attention, spa-like environment.

Running both through the same waiting room, same check-in process, and same flow satisfies neither.

**The separation challenge:**

Cosmetic patients shouldn't wait with medical patients (different expectations, different privacy needs).

Cosmetic consults shouldn't be rushed like medical visits.

Cosmetic tracking (conversion, treatments completed, rebooking) differs from medical tracking.

**What clinIQ does for cosmetic flow:**

Separate workflows: Medical visits and cosmetic visits run different flows. Different stages, different timing, different metrics.

Cosmetic check-in: Private check-in for cosmetic patients. No medical waiting room.

Consult-to-treatment tracking: Cosmetic consultation → treatment scheduled → treatment completed → follow-up scheduled. Conversion tracking.

Injectable tracking: Units used, treatment areas, before/after photos, rebooking intervals.

Membership/package management: For practices with cosmetic memberships or treatment packages, tracking remaining services.

**The revenue opportunity:**

Cosmetic dermatology is high-margin but competitive. Patient experience differentiates. A practice that treats cosmetic patients like VIPs wins.

sections.h2: Biologic Authorization for Psoriasis and Eczema
sections.content:
Biologics for psoriasis (Humira, Cosentyx, Taltz, Skyrizi, etc.) and eczema (Dupixent) require prior authorization and ongoing management.

**The authorization burden:**

Initial auth: Step therapy documentation (failed topicals, phototherapy, conventional systemics), diagnosis confirmation, severity assessment.

Ongoing auth: Annual renewals. Continued medical necessity. Response documentation.

**What goes wrong:**

Auth expires, patient doesn't know, shows up for injection, can't be treated.

Renewal submitted late, gap in coverage, patient misses doses.

Patient transfers from another practice, auth history unknown, starts from scratch.

**What clinIQ does for biologic auth:**

Auth status per patient: Current biologic, auth status, expiration date, doses/refills remaining.

Expiration alerts: 'Dupixent auth expires in 30 days. Patient due for injection in 2 weeks. Renewal needed.'

Step therapy documentation: Prior medication trials documented with dates, duration, and failure reasons. Ready for auth requests.

Dose tracking: For injectables administered in-office, dose tracking for billing and inventory.

**Prior biologic failure:**

Patients switching biologics need documentation of prior biologic failure. clinIQ tracks:
- Prior biologic and duration of use
- Response (partial, none, loss of efficacy)
- Reason for discontinuation
- Washout period if applicable

This documentation supports authorization for the next biologic.

Dermatology runs high volume — 40-60 patients per day per provider isn't unusual. At this volume, flow efficiency determines whether the practice runs smoothly or descends into chaos.

**The high-volume challenge:**

Waiting room fills quickly if flow isn't efficient.

Providers run behind if room turnover is slow.

Staff gets overwhelmed if they can't see what's happening.

**Visit type variation:**

- Routine skin check: 10-15 minutes
- Acne follow-up: 10 minutes
- New patient comprehensive: 30 minutes
- Procedure (excision, biopsy): 15-30 minutes
- Mohs: Separate workflow
- Cosmetic consult: 30-45 minutes
- Injectable treatment: 15-30 minutes

The schedule template must account for this variation.

**What clinIQ does for high-volume flow:**

Room status visibility: Every room — occupied, ready, turnover. Staff sees availability instantly.

Queue by provider: Each provider sees their queue. Who's waiting? Who's ready? No asking.

Wait time alerts: Patient waiting 15+ minutes? Alert before they complain.

Checkout queue: Patients ready for checkout. Prevent bottleneck at front desk.

**The MA workflow:**

In high-volume derm, MAs move fast. Room patient, take vitals, get history, prepare for provider, turn over room for next patient.

clinIQ supports this workflow:
- MA sees their patient queue
- Room readiness visible
- History capture streamlined
- Provider notified when patient ready

Dermatology implementation covers Mohs efficiency, biopsy tracking, cosmetic flow, and general patient flow.

**Week 1: Configuration**

We configure for your practice: visit types, room setup, Mohs workflow (if applicable), biopsy tracking, cosmetic vs medical separation.

**Week 2: Training and Go-Live**

Front desk learns check-in and flow. MAs learn room management. Providers learn their queue. Mohs team learns stage tracking. Cosmetic staff learns their workflow.

Go-live with support.

**Week 3 and Beyond**

Biopsy tracking active. Patient flow visible. Mohs efficiency improving.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Patient flow improvements immediate. Biopsy tracking active.

Month 3: Mohs throughput improvement measurable. Biopsy tracking preventing lost results. Cosmetic flow improving patient experience.

Month 6: Full optimization. Mohs efficiency at new baseline. Zero lost biopsies. Cosmetic conversion improving.

**ROI Math:**

Mohs throughput: 1 additional case/Mohs day × 50 Mohs days/year × $2,000/case = $100,000/year

Biopsy tracking: Risk mitigation (hard to quantify, but one prevented malpractice claim = $500,000+)

Cosmetic conversion: 10% improvement in conversion × cosmetic revenue = variable

Patient flow: Reduced wait times, improved satisfaction, better reviews, more referrals

**Investment:**

Professional: $499/month

Implementation: $750 one-time

faqs.q: How do you track Mohs stages?
faqs.a: Every stage tracked from excision through slide review through decision. Time at each stage visible. Delays surface immediately. Patient status always known.
Every biopsy tracked from collection through patient notification. Pipeline shows status. Aging alerts surface biopsies waiting for results or review. Notification documented.

faqs.q: Can we separate cosmetic and medical flows?
faqs.a: Yes. Different check-in processes, different waiting areas (if you have them), different stage progressions, different metrics. Each population gets appropriate experience.
Auth status per patient per drug. Expiration alerts. Step therapy documentation. Prior biologic failure tracking. Prevents gaps in treatment.

faqs.q: Does this work with pathology labs?
faqs.a: We track biopsy status regardless of lab. If your lab has electronic interfaces, we can integrate for automatic result receipt. Otherwise, manual result entry with tracking.

faqs.q: How do you handle high volume?
faqs.a: Built for high volume. Room status visibility, queue management, wait time tracking. 40-60 patients/day/provider runs smoothly.
Before/after photo tracking by patient and treatment. Photos attached to patient record. Consent documented.
Yes. Simple status updates from any device. Room status changes propagate immediately to all views.
